retrieved_docs.title: Carotid Artery Surgery -- Anatomy and Physiology -- Anatomy of Carotid Artery Surgery: Branches of the Aorta and Brain Blood Circulation
retrieved_docs.content: Carotid artery and branches The common carotid artery (CCA) originates from the aortic arch on the left side and the brachiocephalic trunk on the right side. The CCA then ascends in the neck and bifurcates into the internal carotid artery (ICA) and the external carotid artery (ECA) (see Image. Common Carotid Artery Bifurcation). CCA The CCA runs along the side of the neck and divides into the ECA and ICA at approximately the level of the upper border of the thyroid cartilage (at the C4 vertebral level). The bifurcation is typically located around the level of the cricoid cartilage. ECA The ECA supplies blood to the face, scalp, and neck, with several vital branches: Superior thyroid artery Supplies the thyroid gland and larynx Lingual artery Supplies the tongue and floor of the mouth Facial artery Supply the muscles of the face Occipital artery Supplies the posterior scalp Maxillary and temporal arteries Supply the deep face and scalp The ECA’s branches are essential in surgery because injury to these vessels can lead to significant bleeding, which may complicate the procedure. ICA The ICA does not branch in the neck until it enters the skull through the carotid canal. This artery supplies blood to the brain and parts of the eye. The ICA travels upward through the neck, posterior to the external carotid artery, and enters the skull through the foramen lacerum. Inside the cranial cavity, it gives rise to multiple critical branches, including: Ophthalmic artery Supplies the eye and parts of the orbit Posterior communicating artery Part of the circle of Willis Middle cerebral artery (MCA) Supplies a significant portion of the lateral brain Anterior cerebral artery (ACA) Supplies the medial brain, especially the frontal lobes The ICA is often the focus of surgery in cases of atherosclerotic stenosis because its narrowing leads to a high risk of stroke.

retrieved_docs.title: Carotid Endarterectomy -- Anatomy and Physiology
retrieved_docs.content: Within the superior mediastinum, the arch of the aorta can be found at the level of the sternal angle. Three large vessels originate from the aorta: the brachiocephalic trunk, the left common carotid artery, and the left subclavian artery.  On the right side, the common carotid artery arises as the first branch of the brachiocephalic trunk. Both the left and right common carotids bifurcate into an internal and external carotid artery, classically in the neck at the level of the fourth cervical vertebra (C4), near the superior border of the thyroid cartilage. The deep cervical fascia forms the carotid sheath, surrounding the carotid arteries, internal jugular veins, and vagus nerves. They are found medially to the sternocleidomastoid muscle. The internal carotid artery will continue into the skull to form part of the Circle of Willis and supply blood to the brain and eyes. Branches of the external carotid artery supply blood to the neck and face.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Neck -- Blood Supply and Lymphatics
retrieved_docs.content: The right common carotid artery originates from the brachiocephalic artery, while the left common carotid artery originates directly from the aortic arch. The common carotid bifurcates into the internal carotid and external carotid arteries at the level of the superior portion of the thyroid cartilage. The external carotid artery exits the carotid sheath and supplies the superficial aspect of the face and parts of the neck. It gives off the superior thyroid artery, lingual artery, facial artery, and occipital artery. The internal carotid artery continues into the temporal bone through the carotid canal and joins the circle of Willis to supply the ophthalmic artery, anterior cerebral artery, and the middle cerebral artery. [1] [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Carotid Arteries -- Structure and Function
retrieved_docs.content: The ECA has eight branches, which anastomose with the branches from the contralateral external carotid, allowing for collateral circulation: These branches include Superior thyroid artery Ascending pharyngeal artery Lingual artery Facial artery Occipital artery Posterior auricular artery Maxillary artery Superficial temporal artery

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Anterior: Common Carotid Arteries -- Blood Supply and Lymphatics
retrieved_docs.content: The external carotid arteries have eight major divisions that supply the head, neck, and face. As the external carotid arteries ascend the neck, it gives off a branch called the superior thyroid artery. The superior thyroid artery is vital for the muscles, organs, and structures predominantly inferior to the hyoid bone like the thyroid gland, infrahyoid muscles, and sternocleidomastoid muscle, but also the larynx above the hyoid bone. The second branch from the external carotid arteries is the ascending pharyngeal arteries. These arteries supply the middle ear, pharynx part of the oral cavity, prevertebral muscles and ascend to supply the meninges. The next branch that arises is the lingual artery that supplies the floor of the mouth and tongue. As the external carotid artery approach the mandible, it gives off the facial artery. The facial artery has multiple regions of the face, palate part of the oral cavity and tonsils, and submandibular glands that it supplies. The external carotid supplies the scalp through the next branch, which is the occipital artery. The posterior auricular artery is the last branch before the external carotid artery terminates into end branches. This artery is important in supplying the ear, scalp, cranial never VII, and parts of the scalp. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Doppler Extra-Cranial Carotid Assessment, Protocols, and Interpretation -- Anatomy and Physiology
retrieved_docs.content: The most frequent anatomical variant involves a common origin of the brachiocephalic artery and the left CCA from the aortic arch. The CCAs bifurcate into the external carotid (ECA) and internal carotid (ICA) arteries at the upper border of the thyroid. The carotid bulb marks the site of bifurcation and the ICA origin. [6] The ICA is generally posterior and lateral to the ECA and has a larger caliber (see Image. Carotid Bifurcation on Ultrasonography). The ECA supplies the face and neck musculature, tapering distally as it branches into the extracranial regions. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Anterior: Common Carotid Arteries -- Blood Supply and Lymphatics
retrieved_docs.content: The common carotid arteries itself do not supply blood to the body, but its branches supply the head and neck regions from the external and internal carotid arteries. Following the bifurcation of the common carotid arteries, the internal carotid arteries go into the brain and supply the brain with blood via its terminal branches and contributing to the formation of the circle of Willis. The internal carotid artery branches into the anterior cerebral artery, and this supplies the frontal lobe medially and superiorly. The middle cerebral branch of the internal carotid supplies the lateral portion of the frontal lobes and the superior half of the temporal lobes. The blood supplies of the middle cerebral arteries are so vast that it even supplies some of the deep nuclei and the anterior portion of the parietal lobes.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Anterior: Common Carotid Arteries -- Physiologic Variants
retrieved_docs.content: The common carotid artery coming off the aorta on the left and the right common carotid artery usually comes off the brachiocephalic trunk. There are variations where the right common carotid artery comes directly from the aorta. Even rarer is the right common carotid artery originating from the right subclavian artery. [11] The most common site for bifurcation of the common carotid artery is at C4, but the use of the C4 level as a landmark may be inconsistent in different individuals. The bifurcation may be slightly higher or lower than C4. [12] The size of the internal carotid artery and external carotid artery can vary from person to person, but usually, the internal carotid artery tends to be slightly bigger. External carotid artery branches have many variations. The order of the eight branches coming off of the external carotid artery can vary greatly, but the region of blood supply stays consistent.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Internal Carotid Arteries -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Proper circulation of oxygenated blood is such an incredibly important function of the human body that it is included in the “ABCDE’s” (airway, breathing, circulation, disability, and exposure) of the primary survey conducted in the emergent clinical setting. [1] Adequate circulation throughout the body is crucial for supplying oxygenated blood to vital organs. Some of the most important conduits of blood flow are the carotid arteries, which consist of the common carotids, external carotids, and the internal carotids.

retrieved_docs.title: Carotid Artery Stenting -- Anatomy and Physiology
retrieved_docs.content: The common carotid artery arises from the aortic arch (left) or the brachiocephalic trunk (right). It divides into internal and external carotid arteries. The internal carotid artery provides blood to the brain while the external carotid artery provides blood to the face, scalp and the neck. The internal and external carotid arteries form collaterals at several locations. This is helpful for the maintenance of blood flow through collateral circulation in case either artery becomes occluded. The aortic arch can be divided into three anatomic types. To simplify, an imaginary horizontal line is drawn through the top of the aortic arch, and imaginary parallel lines marking the origins of the great vessels coming off the arch are drawn for comparison. For a type 1 arch, all great vessels originate close to the imaginary horizontal line drawn through the top of the aortic arch. If the origins of all great vessels fall within the second parallel line, this is considered a type 2 aortic arch. For a type 3 aortic arch, the origins of all great vessels fall within the third parallel line. The angulation, as related to the type of aortic arch, has important implications for CAS.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Carotid Arteries -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The head and neck region obtain the majority of its blood supply via the carotid and also vertebral arteries. This activity primarily focuses on the in-depth orientation of the carotid arteries, including their anatomical course, branches and also the area of distribution. The carotid arteries are the primary vessels supplying blood to the brain and face. [1] [2] The right common carotid artery (RCCA) originates in the neck from the brachiocephalic artery while the left common carotid artery (LCCA) arises in the thorax from the arch of the aorta. [3] Furthermore, both right and left common carotid arteries bifurcate in the neck at the level of the carotid sinus into the internal carotid artery (ICA), which supplies the brain, and the external carotid artery (ECA), which supplies the neck and face. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Internal Carotid Arteries -- Structure and Function
retrieved_docs.content: As blood is oxygenated in the lungs and enters the left atrium, it traverses the mitral valves into the left ventricle where it is pushed out through the aortic valve into the ascending aorta. The first major branch of the aorta that blood will circulate through is the brachiocephalic artery, which then gives off the right common carotid as it turns into the right subclavian artery. As blood traverses the aortic arch, it reaches the second major branch of the aorta, the left common carotid artery. The common carotids then traverse up into the neck through the carotid sheath, where they finally bifurcate into the external and internal carotid arteries at the level of the carotid sinus and the fourth cervical vertebra (C4). [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Internal Carotid Arteries -- Nerves
retrieved_docs.content: The internal carotid artery also gives off the ophthalmic artery, which gives off numerous collateral branches to supply the optic nerve. Also, the ophthalmic artery's first major branch, the central retinal artery, travels within the optic nerve to supply the retina. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Internal Maxillary Arteries -- Structure and Function
retrieved_docs.content: The internal maxillary artery, simply known as the maxillary artery, is a terminal branch of the external carotid artery (ECA); which itself is a branch of the bilateral common carotid arteries at the C4 vertebral level where the common carotids bifurcate into the internal and external carotids. The external carotid supplies blood to structures in the superficial face, and superior superficial cranial structures. The branches of the external carotid artery include the superior thyroid artery, ascending pharyngeal artery, lingual artery, facial artery, occipital artery, posterior auricular artery, and the two terminal branches are the maxillary artery and superficial temporalis artery.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Carotid Sheath -- Blood Supply and Lymphatics
retrieved_docs.content: The 2 vascular structures within the carotid sheath are the common carotid artery, which becomes the external carotid artery, and the internal jugular vein. At the base of the carotid sheath, the common carotid artery arises directly on the left side of the arch of the aorta and directly on the right side of the brachiocephalic trunk. Proximal to its bifurcation, there are no branches from the common carotid artery. The bifurcation of the common carotid artery commonly occurs at the level of the thyroid cartilage (C4 level).

retrieved_docs.title: Angiography -- Anatomy and Physiology
retrieved_docs.content: The aorta starts at the aortic root, which in healthy individuals measures up to 4.3 cm. [3] The aorta gives off the left and right main coronary arteries at the level of the sinus of Valsalva. It then continues as the ascending aorta, before turning into the aortic arch. Three important branches originate from the arch: The brachiocephalic trunk divides to form the right common carotid and right subclavian arteries. The left common carotid artery further divides to form the internal and external carotid arteries. The internal carotid artery, together with the vertebral artery, will form the circle of Wilis, while the external carotid artery gives off the superior thyroid artery, the ascending pharyngeal artery, and the lingual artery. Other branches of the external carotid artery include the facial artery, the occipital artery, the posterior auricular artery, the maxillary artery, and the superficial temporal artery The left subclavian artery

This integrated network supports comprehensive assessment and management of precerebral arterial disease, its neurologic manifestations, and systemic consequences.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 1 -- Community 231
retrieved_docs.content: surgical removal of atherosclerotic plaque—endarterectomy of head‑and‑neck vessels (استئصال باطنة الشريان)—is indicated in the context of arterial thrombosis or embolism. The intracranial arterial structure can reveal intracerebral atherosclerotic changes, providing an indirect site for such procedures. Together, these entities illustrate the interconnected pathophysiology of precerebral arterial disease, basilar artery involvement, cerebral infarction, transient ischemic events, clinical presentation, and both medical and surgical management strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 2 -- Community 1026
retrieved_docs.content: The Structure of intracranial artery encompasses the arteria basilaris as a subordinate component, indicating that the basilar artery is a child element within the overall architecture of intracranial arterial structures. This anatomical framework serves as a common site for the finding described as تصلب عصيدي في المخ, reflecting the presence of intracranial atherosclerotic disease within these vessels. Surgical management of vascular pathology in the head and neck region often involves an endarterectomy; vessel of head and neck, a procedure that is indirectly related to the Structure of intracranial artery because interventions on extracranial vessels can affect or be associated with intracranial arterial anatomy. The endarterectomy; vessel of head and neck itself is classified under the broader procedural category استئصال باطنة الشريان (arterial endarterectomy), establishing a hierarchical relationship in which the specific head‑and‑neck endarterectomy is a subset of general arterial endarterectomy. Understanding these anatomical, pathological, and procedural connections is essential for accurate diagnosis, risk assessment, and the planning of appropriate surgical or endovascular treatments involving intracranial and extracranial arteries.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 2 -- Community 891

This intracranial arterial structure is a recognized site for the finding of تصلب عصيدي في المخ (intracranial atherosclerosis). Although direct surgical access to intracranial arteries is limited, endarterectomy of vessels of the head and neck is considered an indirect procedural approach that may affect the structure of intracranial artery. Understanding the relationships among the basilar artery, intracranial atherosclerotic disease, and head‑and‑neck endarterectomy is important for comprehensive cerebrovascular assessment and treatment planning.

The basilar artery is also the anatomic location where transient cerebral ischemia (نوبة إقفارية عابرة) occurs, representing a transient ischemic attack. Occlusion or stenosis of precerebral arteries encompasses basilar‑artery narrowing (تضيُّق الشريان القاعدي) and basilar‑artery thrombosis (خثار الشريان القاعدي), both linked to cerebral atherosclerosis and to the development of احتشاء دماغي. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 2 -- Community 995
retrieved_docs.content: The Structure of intracranial artery is identified as the anatomical site where تصلب عصيدي في المخ (intracranial atherosclerotic disease) can be found. In addition, this arterial structure is regarded as an indirect procedure site for endarterectomy; vessel of head and neck, indicating that surgical planning for endarterectomy may involve the intracranial arterial region even when the primary target is a head‑and‑neck vessel. The entity endarterectomy; vessel of head and neck is itself a specific instance of the broader surgical technique استئصال باطنة الشريان (endarterectomy of the arterial intima), reflecting a hierarchical relationship in which the head‑and‑neck endarterectomy procedure is a subcategory of general arterial intimal removal. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 2 -- Community 771
retrieved_docs.content: The Structure of intracranial artery encompasses the arteria basilaris as a child component, indicating that the basilar artery is a sub‑structure within the broader intracranial arterial system. This anatomical structure is identified as a finding site for تصلب عصيدي في المخ (intracranial arterial sclerosis), highlighting its clinical relevance in the assessment of cerebrovascular disease. Endarterectomy; vessel of head and neck is described as an indirect procedure site of the Structure of intracranial artery, meaning that surgical removal of the arterial intima in head‑and‑neck vessels may be performed in relation to this intracranial arterial region. The procedure Endarterectomy; vessel of head and neck has a parent relationship with استئصال باطنة الشريان (general endarterectomy), placing the specific head‑and‑neck endarterectomy as a specialized form of the broader endarterectomy technique. Finally, استئصال باطنة الشريان has member cases classified under حالات الخثار والانصمام الشريانية (SMQ) (arterial thrombosis and embolism cases), indicating that endarterectomy is a therapeutic option for patients presenting with arterial thrombotic or embolic events. Together, these relationships delineate how the intracranial arterial anatomy, associated pathological findings, and endarterectomy procedures are interconnected in the diagnosis and management of cerebrovascular and arterial thrombo‑embolic conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 1 -- Community 175
retrieved_docs.content: in cerebraal infarct,” highlights that precerebral arterial narrowing does not always produce overt infarction. Asymptomatic basilar‑artery occlusion may be interpreted within the context of “General wellbeing” and can manifest subtly as syncope (“غشي”), a transient clinical sign that also connects to “Transient cerebral ischemia” (a child of “Circulatory Diseases and Syndromes of Nervous System”). The term “Transitory” links both syncope and transient cerebral ischemia, indicating that brief, reversible ischemic episodes (transient ischemic attacks نوبة إقفارية عابرة) may precede permanent infarction. Imaging and pathological findings such as intracranial atherosclerosis (“تصلب عصيدي في المخ”) are mapped from precerebral arterial disease and are located in the “Structure of intracranial artery.” This structural involvement provides an indirect procedural site for endarterectomy of head‑and‑neck vessels (“endarterectomy; vessel of head and neck”), which falls under the broader

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 1 -- Community 199
retrieved_docs.content: Therapeutic procedures for arteries (excluding the aorta) form a hierarchical group that includes carotid endarterectomy, generic arterial endarterectomy, aneurysm resection, pulmonary artery banding, and percutaneous coronary intervention performed through the skin. Carotid endarterectomy is a specific instance of arterial endarterectomy, and the endarterectomy of head‑and‑neck vessels is classified as a subprocedure under the broader arterial endarterectomy category. The anatomical structure of the intracranial artery serves as a site where intracranial arterial atherosclerosis (تصلب عصيدي في المخ) can be identified, and it is also considered an indirect procedural site for endarterectomy of head‑and‑neck vessels. This integrated view of vascular interventions and their related anatomical sites supports comprehensive planning of patient care and a clearer understanding of how different arterial procedures are related within the vascular therapeutic landscape.

Structural consequences of precerebral arterial narrowing include intracranial arterial sclerosis (تصلب عصيدي في المخ), a finding site of the Structure of intracranial artery. This sclerosis is directly linked to the underlying occlusive process and represents a target for surgical intervention. Endarterectomy of the vessel of head and neck (endarterectomy; vessel of head and neck) is identified as an indirect procedural site for addressing plaque within the intracranial artery. This procedure falls under the broader category استئصال باطنة الشريان (arterial endarterectomy), which is indicated for cases encompassed by حالات الخثار والانصمام الشريانية (SMQ). Overall, the interrelationships among precerebral arterial occlusion/stenosis, basilar artery narrowing or thrombosis, embolic and thrombotic cerebral infarcts, transient ischemic

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 1 -- Community 147
retrieved_docs.content: event) and whose parent is transient cerebral ischemia, also manifested clinically as نوبة إقفارية عابرة (TIA). The SMQ “أمراض وعائية إقْفارِيّة في الجهاز العصبي المركزي” and the SMQ “حالات الخثار والانصمام الشريانية” enumerate these cerebrovascular events, including basilar artery stenosis (تضيُّق الشريان القاعدي), basilar artery thrombosis (خثار الشريان القاعدي), and basilar artery embolism (Embolie; arteria basilaris), which is a subtype of انصمام الشريان المخي (cerebral artery embolism). The intracranial arterial structure, particularly the arteria basilaris, serves as a finding site for precerebral arterial occlusion/stenosis, transient cerebral ischemia, and intracerebral atherosclerosis (تصلب عصيدي في المخ). Atherosclerotic changes in the brain’s arterial wall are linked to precerebral arterial blockage.
